figures.caption: Fig. 4: DAGverse-1 data card. The left panel summarizes key statistics, including node and edge summaries and the most frequent domain tags. The right panel visualizes the node and edge distribution by source, a domain word cloud, and a top domain count plot. We omit two general tags “Causal Inference” and “Machine Learning” in domain visualizations.
Fig. 1: Constructing a semantic DAG (Appendix A) from a scientific paper [25].
Fig. 3: Validation interface artifacts used by experts. Left: reconstructed DAG view for structure checking. Right: evidence panel for node and edge grounding checks.
Table 3: Comparison between our dataset and existing causal DAGs datasets.

Fig. 5: A single semantic DAG in DAGverse can be used in different ways. Using the cirrhosis example [25], we demonstrate 3 tasks: text-to-graph recovery from grounded evidence, graph-to-text generation of a coherent causal narrative, and causal question answering over graph structure and semantics.

Table 4: End-to-end pipeline funnel from raw metadata to validated outputs. For each source, the percentage in parentheses is the retention rate at that step relative to the total number of papers in that source’s metadata.

Table 1: We compare DAGverse-Pipeline with popular open source and propretary VLMs. “Pred.” is the number of figures labeld as DAGs, “True DAG” are actual DAGs. “DC”, “SD”, “EA”, and “E2E” are calculated with respect to each model’s correct examples. Among all models, DAGverse-Pipeline has the highest retention rate, without highest number of examples kept.
Fig. 2: Overview of DAGverse-Pipeline. Paper collection can be updated routinely.
